frozen_components: Backbone LLM (Qwen2.5-Base, fine-tuned but weights are fixed during Thinking States training)
architecture.summary: Thinking States augments a backbone LLM (Qwen2.5) with two lightweight modules: a Thinking Block (single-layer autoregressive Transformer decoder) that generates natural-language reasoning tokens from deep representations, and a Compression Block (Transformer encoder with pooling) that maps variable-length thought sequences into fixed-size states. During inference, the model processes input chunks iteratively: inject the previous state into shallow-layer embeddings, forward through the LLM to deep layers, decode thoughts from deep representations, compress them into a state, and repeat. During training, target reasoning sequences (paired with each chunk) are teacher-forced to inject gold states, enabling fully parallel training without backpropagation through time. States never extend the context window; they condition future processing via residual injection at shallow layers.
trained_components: Thinking Block (autoregressive decoder for natural-language reasoning tokens)
Compression Block (Transformer encoder mapping thoughts to fixed-size states)
Injection and extraction points (residual connections from deep to shallow layers)

biblio.title: Latent Reasoning with Supervised Thinking States

claims.statement: On State Tracking Parity task trained on N=10, Thinking States achieves 98.37% accuracy
On 2-Hop QA Full Knowledge, Thinking States achieves 54.91% accuracy with 1.19x speedup vs CoT (54.79% accuracy, 1x speedup)
On GSM8K-style math problems, Thinking States achieves 42.22% accuracy with 2.66x speedup vs CoT (60.50% accuracy, 1x speedup)
Thinking States outperforms Coconut on GSM by approximately 10 percentage points (42.22% vs 32.65%)
Length generalization on Parity: trained on N≤10, Thinking States achieves 100% accuracy on N=40, vs CoT 64.38%

Training cost comparison: BPTT incurs linearly increasing wall-clock time with recurrent steps, whereas Thinking States maintains close to constant training time due to teacher forcing, preventing cost penalties at 10+ steps
Approximately 12% of queries correctly solved by Thinking States are not solved by CoT, with CoT exhibiting hallucinated reasoning steps or overly complex intermediate computations

State ambiguity: Thinking States fails when the quantity of interest is specified only in the final clause of a query; prepending the question to the start of the prompt zero-shot improves accuracy from 42.22% to 48.65% on error cases, suggesting limitation stems from causal processing order rather than the mechanism itself
